vision:
Climate-resilient city; 
Net Zero GHG emissions by 2070
goals:
Reduce GHG emission intensity of its GDP by 45% by 2030;
reach net zero by 2070
climateRisks:
Extreme Heat
Urban Flooding
Air Pollution
riskFindings:
Extreme Heat
Ahmedabad runs high risk of extreme heat events; temperatures rising at 0.15°C/decade (1970-2020). Heat wave events increased by 4 events (1951-2019) and projected to increase by 5-30 events by 2070. Land surface temperature varies between 29°C to 47°C. Vulnerable areas include Amraiwadi, Bapunagar, Viratnagar and industrial zones.

Urban Flooding
4.3% of city area affected by urban flooding/waterlogging, impacting ~9.5% of population (~0.7 million people). Vulnerable areas include Naranpura, Khokhra, Bodakdev, Paldi, Maninagar, Vasna and Vatva. Residents within 250m of 166 waterlogged/flood points face elevated risk.

Air Pollution
Ahmedabad is listed as a non-attainment city by CPCB. Key pollutants are PM10 and PM2.5. Road dust, domestic sector, construction and point sources (industries) are major contributors.

strategies.info:
Improved air quality, enhanced public health, increased economic opportunities through innovation and clean technologies, strengthened energy and water security, and the creation of green jobs are few of the positive outcomes expected to be achieved.
It is estimated that the proposed actions will generate 479,690 green jobs through renewable energy implementation in buildings, electric mobility, and municipal facilities.

vision: Mitigating GHG Emissions, Net zero and climate-resilient Bengaluru by 2070
goals: Reduing GHG emissions by 16% by 2030, 26% by 2040 and 56% by 2050.
climateRisks:
Urban Flooding
Urban Heat
Drought
Thunderstorms & Lightning
Air Pollution
riskFindings:
Urban Flooding
Total area of ~180 sq. km in the city is at risk of floods, hosting ~21% of population. Flood risk categorised into High (6% of population), Medium (4%) and Low (11%) risk zones. Peripheral zones — Yelahanka (North), Mahadevapura (East), RR Nagar (Southwest) — are highest priority areas.

Urban Heat
Mean air temperature rising at 0.23°C/decade since 1975; increase accelerated to ~0.5°C/decade in last decade. Number of heat wave incidents peaking; since 1975, city faces ~1.5 additional 'caution' days of high heat index every two years. Areas such as Peenya, Hebbal and Nagarabhavi show higher land surface temperatures (35–36°C).

Drought
84% of the Cauvery-Ponnaiyar Basin area is in the 'very high risk' category for drought. Region is 'overexploited' for groundwater. Since 2000, a further 31% of city area has seen decline in groundwater recharge potential.

Thunderstorms & Lightning
City gets ~44 days of thunderstorms per year on average. Karnataka saw a 68.74% rise in lightning strikes in 2021-22.

Air Pollution
Annual PM10 averages vary between 60-123 µg/m³. PM2.5 bordering CPCB limits at 30-40 µg/m³ per year.

strategies.strategy:
1) 40% of new appliance technology will be of high-range energy efficiency, 42% of new residential buildings and 48% of new commercial buildings will adopt insulated walls and roofs, improved window technology, and improved building services design, 100% transition from solid fuel to clean cooking fuel  by 2030;  
2) 92% of the new households in the city (from 2019) should be in Transit-Oriented Development areas by 2050. The combined share of public transport (PT) and non-motorised transport (NMT) in total no. of trips to be 75% by 2030, 80% by 2040, 85% by 2050.  90% of cars and motorcycles, 75% of buses to be powered by cleaner fuels by 2050. 47% of total freight vehicles to be powered by cleaner fuels by 2050.;
3)  Achieve 100% segregation of waste by 2025 and achieve over 90% diversion of waste from landfills by 2050 (60% by 2030 & 75% by 2040). Recycling of paper & plastic waste to be >60% by 2030, >75% by 2040 & >90% by 2050.   Achieve about 25% gas capture from scientific landfill by 2030, 50% by 2040 & 75% by 2050. 
4)  To achieve 40% reduction in PM10 concentration and 30% reduction in PM2.5 concentration by 2026 from 2017-18 levels as per revised targets prescribed by NCAP.  30% of commercial and institutional buildings complying with WHO Indoor air quality standards and guidelines by 2030. Preparation of a roadmap for a comprehensive health action plan tackling risks due to air pollution for 2025-2035. To procure 13 new CAAQMS and transition the existing manual station to CAAQMS systems by 2035. 
5)  100% households to have access to water and sanitation infrastructure and services, which are a combination of networked and decentralised systems.  By 2050, aim to reduce non-revenue water to 15%. Achieve reuse of treated wastewater to a minimum of 50% by 2030 and 90% by 2050. 40% of city's surface area to become permeable by 2040 by reclaiming ecologically sensitive spaces and adopting nature-based solutions. Convert 75% of the city's footpaths to permeable surface material by 2050.  95% wastewater treatment with a combination of centralised and decentralised system with about 61% low-carbon wastewater treatment technologies by 2050 . 
6) Prepare and adopt the RMP 2041 and set up the Master Plan Coordination Committee by 2025. All spatial plans and DCRs to adopt climate action as a lens by 2030.  Conserve and restore all blue-green networks in the city. All urban infrastructure schemes and projects should adhere to disaster resilience standards. Increase the city's tree cover (canopy) from the baseline by 10% by 2030 and 20% by 2040. Increase the city's green (vegetation) cover and permeable surfaces to 40% of the city's area by 2040, to tackle flood- and heat-related disaster risk reduction. Convert 75% of the city's footpaths to permeable surface material by 2050. Operationalise the (recently re-constituted) BMC by 2023 and prepare the People's Biodiversity Register by 2025. By 2050, provide safe and affordable housing for 100% urban poor and vulnerable groups in locations having access to public transport stops/stations within a 5-minute walk. By 2050, increase the city's publicly accessible per capita open space from the existing 2.2 sq. m to 6 sq. m 
7) A comprehensive and regularly updated data repository for the city. A comprehensive disaster management plan.  Empowered ward committees for better localised preparedness for, response to, and recovery from disasters. Reduction in loss of life, livelihoods, and assets due to climate and environmental hazards..
strategies.info:
Inclusivity of the process: Engagement with a wide range of communities and stakeholders. 
Inclusivity of the policy: Fairness and accessibility in design and delivery.
 Inclusivity of the impact: Wider benefits of action as equitably distributed as possible

goals:
Carbon neutrality by 2050;
Water balance by 2050
climateRisks:
Flash Floods / Surface Floods
Coastal Floods
Heatwave / Urban Heat Island
Water Scarcity
Sea Level Rise
Severe Winds
riskFindings:
Flash Floods / Surface Floods
At a 5-year return period (20cm rainfall), 29.1% of GCC area is at risk of flood. At 25-year return period (38cm rainfall), 46% of GCC area is at risk. At 100-year return period (47cm rainfall), 56.5% of GCC area is at risk of flood.

Coastal Floods
Coastal communities and fishing villages face coastal inundation even during non-monsoon days due to high tides. Sea level rise projections compound risk for low-lying coastal areas.

Heatwave / Urban Heat Island
Increasing frequency of heat waves; urban heat island effect intensifying in densely built areas.

Water Scarcity
Delayed and irregular monsoon alongside reduction in rainfall and water bodies increasing water stress. Groundwater depletion a growing concern.

vision: 'Net Zero' & 'Climate Resilient' Jamnagar by 2050, with two key objectives: Mitigation and Adaptation.
climateRisks:
Urban Flooding
Storm Surge / Cyclone Risk
Seismic Vulnerability
Drought / Water Scarcity
riskFindings:
Urban Flooding
Composite flood risk mapping identifies 9% of city area at high risk (Ward 10 and near Railway Station in Ward 1), 18% at moderate risk (Wards 4, 9, 12) and 73% at low risk. City's low elevation (20m MSL) and erratic rainfall makes it highly flood-prone. River dam overflow contributed to 2021 flash floods.

Cyclone / Storm Surge Risk
Coastal location makes Jamnagar highly vulnerable; over 120 cyclones from the Arabian Sea have impacted the Gujarat coastal region in the past century. Wind speeds can exceed 55 m/sec (almost 200 km/h). Storm surges cause saline water intrusion rendering fertile land unproductive.

Seismic Vulnerability
Jamnagar falls under Zone-IV (0.24g) of seismic vulnerability, translating to moderate risk of earthquakes with shaking intensity 2.3× the Design Basis Earthquake standard.

Drought / Water Scarcity
SPEI data reveals 3 severe drought events since 1951. Minimum temperature rise of 0.1–1.5°C observed; frequency of hot days increasing to 30 and hot nights to 24. Saltwater intrusion in estuaries due to reduced freshwater flows is increasing.

strategies.action:
1) Increase share of renewable energy (especially rooftop solar); Promote energy-efficient appliances and systems (LEDs, BLDC fans); Improve energy efficiency in residential, commercial and industrial sectors; Reduce AT&C losses in electricity distribution; Promote green building codes (ECBC, Eco Niwas Samhita); Increase energy-efficient street lighting coverage
2) Increase adoption of electric vehicles (EVs); Promote CNG-based transport systems; Improve public transport coverage and ridership; Develop non-motorized transport (NMT) like walking and cycling; Reduce dependency on private fossil-fuel vehicles; Improve air quality and reduce PM levels
3) Achieve waste segregation at source (wet/dry); Increase recycling and recovery of dry waste; Improve processing of wet waste (composting/biogas); Reduce landfill dependency; Implement waste-to-energy solutions; Improve scientific landfill management and remediation; 
4) Improve water resource management and reduce NRW (losses); Promote rainwater harvesting across the city; Develop flood and stormwater management systems; Rejuvenate water bodies and wetlands; Increase urban green cover and biodiversity; Address urban heat island effects
strategies.intervention:
1) Incentivize rooftop solar installations in households and industries; Implement energy audits and efficiency retrofits in buildings; Enforce building energy codes and compliance mechanisms; Replace conventional lighting with LED-based smart systems; Strengthen grid infrastructure to reduce transmission losses; Promote public awareness programs on energy conservation; 
2) Provide charging infrastructure for EVs across the city; Encourage fleet transition (autos, buses) to EV/CNG; Expand and optimize bus routes and frequency; Develop pedestrian-friendly pathways and cycling tracks; Implement clean air action plans and monitoring systems; Promote shared mobility systems
3) Establish segregation systems at household and community level; Strengthen collection, transport and processing infrastructure; Develop waste-to-energy plants and composting units; Promote circular economy practices and reuse systems; Implement strict monitoring and compliance mechanisms; Conduct awareness campaigns for waste minimization
4) Implement "Sponge City" approach for flood mitigation; Construct check dams and improve drainage networks; Promote reuse and recycling of wastewater; Protect and restore wetlands and natural ecosystems; Increase tree cover (target ~2.5 trees per capita); Integrate climate-resilient urban planning policies
strategies.info:
Focus on resilience (protecting vulnerable populations)
Alignment with SDGs and inclusive planning

vision: Net Zero emissions by 2050; Climate-resilient and sustainable city
goals:
Reduce emissions by 10,79,834 MTCO₂e by 2050; 
Achieve Net Zero through mitigation and adaptation pathways
riskFindings:
Urban Flooding
43% of the city's total area falls under high to very high flood risk. The city has no storm-water drainage network coverage, leaving it highly vulnerable. 23.85 sq. km classified as high risk and 0.54 sq. km as very high risk. 44 Anganwadi centres at high risk and 126 at medium risk. City has implemented a functional Early Warning System (EWS) to reduce impacts.

strategies.intervention:
1) Promote solar rooftop systems and renewable energy projects; Implement energy audits and retrofitting programs; Enforce energy conservation building codes (ECBC, ENS); Promote energy-efficient appliances and lighting systems; Strengthen grid efficiency and reduce transmission losses
2) Develop EV charging infrastructure across the city; Encourage transition of public and shared transport to EV/CNG
Improve bus systems and route coverage; Develop pedestrian pathways and cycling infrastructure; Implement air pollution monitoring and control measures
3) Strengthen waste collection and segregation systems; Develop waste processing and recycling facilities; Promote composting and waste-to-energy initiatives; Implement scientific landfill and remediation practices; Conduct public awareness and behavioral change programs
4) Implement flood risk mitigation and drainage improvement systems; Restore and protect water bodies and river systems; Promote reuse and recycling of wastewater; Increase urban green cover and biodiversity initiatives; Adopt climate-resilient urban planning practices
strategies.info:
Emissions expected to increase 2.6× by 2050
Transportation emissions expected to rise significantly (~37%)
Flood risk is very high (43% area high to very high risk)
Green cover declined significantly ? major focus area

vision: Achieve net-zero emissions and climate resilience by 2047 through a multisectoral low-carbon transition, clean energy adoption, and sustainable development practices
goals:
2030: 31.8% reduction from BAU
2040: 56% reduction from BAU
2047: 83.8% reduction from BAU
climateRisks:
Urban Flooding
Extreme Heat
Coastal Risks
Water Stress
riskFindings:
Urban Flooding
Coastal city with high exposure to flooding from extreme rainfall events and storm surges.

Extreme Heat
Rising temperatures and urban heat island effects increasing heat stress across city.

Coastal Risks
Vulnerable to sea level rise, storm surge and coastal inundation given location on Bay of Bengal coast.

Water Stress
Pressure on water resources from competing demands and projected climate variability.

strategies.info:
27 strategies and 81 climate actions across sectors
Investment required: ?44,226 million by 2047
~3,800+ green jobs expected
Focus on both mitigation + adaptation

vision: Achieve Net Zero emissions and Climate Resilient Leh by 2047
riskFindings:
Urban Floods
1.86 sq km (42.5%) of Leh's developed area is currently at high flood risk
16,052 people (~38% of Leh's population) currently live in high-risk flood zones
Over 90% of Leh's population (38,000+ people) live with medium to high flood risk

Water Scarcity
7% of Leh's residents already live in zones with low groundwater levels
Of these low groundwater regions, more than 30% are government buildings and 11% are industrial areas

strategies.strategy:
1) Reduce emissions from energy consumption and improve efficiency
2) Reduce emissions from transport and promote sustainable mobility
3) Minimize emissions from waste and improve waste handling
4) Enhance resilience to floods and water scarcity
strategies.action:
1) Reduce electricity and fuel consumption through efficiency measures; Promote renewable energy (solar power, rooftop systems); Improve energy performance of buildings and infrastructure; Reduce dependence on fossil fuels for heating and electricity; 
2) Reduce fuel consumption in transport sector; Promote electric vehicles (EVs); Improve public transport and reduce private vehicle dependency; Reduce vehicular emissions contributing to air pollution
3) Reduce waste generation and landfill dependency; Improve waste segregation and processing; Reduce methane emissions from waste
4) Improve water availability and reduce groundwater stress; Address urban flooding risks (affecting ~90% population); Revive natural water systems (springs, recharge zones); Improve stormwater management infrastructure
strategies.intervention:
1) Deploy solar energy farms and rooftop solar systems; Promote energy-efficient appliances and lighting systems; Implement building efficiency improvements and retrofits; Encourage low-energy consumption practices
2) Transition to electric mobility systems (cars, taxis, public transport); Improve transport planning and connectivity; Promote clean fuels and low-emission vehicles; Encourage shared mobility and reduced travel demand
3) Strengthen waste collection and processing systems; Promote scientific waste management practices; Implement waste treatment and methane reduction methods; Encourage reuse and recycling practices
4) Develop stormwater drainage systems (84+ km planned); Implement groundwater recharge and spring revival programs; Establish flood detention and risk management zones; Promote water-efficient infrastructure and reuse systems
strategies.info:
~90% population at flood risk
Focus strongly on adaptation (water + floods)
Tourism-driven economy ? seasonal emissions variation
Emissions expected to increase ~3.5× by 2047

vision: Net Zero and Climate Resilient Mumbai aligned with 1.5°C pathway
goals:
50% emission reduction by 2030
Achieve carbon neutrality (Net Zero) by 2050
Align with Paris Agreement (1.5°C scenario)
organizations:
Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC)
Government of Maharashtra
C40 Cities
World Resources Institute India (WRI India)
Maharashtra Pollution Control Board (MPCB)
climateRisks:
Urban Heat
Urban Flooding
Landslides
Coastal Risks
Air Pollution
riskFindings:
Urban Heat
City experiencing higher temperatures and variable heat stress driven by urban heat island (UHI) effects. Areas with dense informal settlements and limited green cover are most exposed.

Urban Flooding
Flood-prone areas routinely waterlogged during monsoons and submerged for several hours during extreme rainfall events (EREs). By 2080, likelihood of urban floods like the 2005 event is projected to more than double. 2005 deluge caused financial losses estimated at USD 1,100 million, of which ~USD 245 million borne by marginalised population. By 2070s, Mumbai projected to be among the top Asian cities in population exposed to coastal flooding.

Landslides
Landslide-prone areas experience heavy loss and damages during EREs, especially where retaining walls are weak.

Coastal Risks
Upward trend of cyclonic events in the Arabian Sea due to warming seas increasing coastal risks. Coastal communities face inundation during non-monsoon days from high tides. Sea level rise projections compound this risk.

Air Pollution
Increasing traffic congestion, unregulated construction, and mismanaged solid waste elevate air pollution risks and health impacts, particularly for vulnerable communities.

strategies.sector:
1) Energy & Buildings
2) Sustainable Mobility
3) Waste Management
4) Urban Greening & Biodiversity
5) Air Quality
6) Urban Flooding & Water Resource Management
strategies.strategy:
1) Reduce emissions from buildings and transition to clean energy
2) Reduce transport emissions and improve sustainable mobility
3) Reduce emissions from waste and improve sustainability
4) Enhance carbon sinks and ecological resilience
5) Improve air quality and reduce pollution
6) Enhance resilience to flooding and water stress
strategies.action:
1) Increase renewable energy (solar rooftops, distributed RE); Improve energy efficiency in residential & commercial buildings; Promote net-zero and green buildings; Reduce electricity demand and carbon intensity
2) Increase public transport share (already high ~76%); Promote electric mobility (EV buses, vehicles); Reduce private vehicle dependency; Improve multi-modal transport integration
3) Achieve 100% waste segregation; Reduce landfill dependency (currently high); Improve waste processing and recycling; Reduce methane emissions from waste
4) Increase tree cover and green spaces; Protect mangroves and forests; Improve urban biodiversity
5) Reduce PM2.5 & PM10 levels; Control vehicular and industrial emissions; Improve air quality monitoring systems
6) Reduce urban flooding risks (major issue in Mumbai); Improve stormwater drainage systems; Enhance water supply and reuse systems
strategies.intervention:
1) Implement ECBC & green building regulations; Promote solar rooftop and clean grid transition; Retrofit existing buildings for efficiency; Deploy energy-efficient appliances and systems
2) Electrify bus fleet and transport systems; Expand metro, suburban rail, and integrated mobility; Promote walking & cycling infrastructure (NMT); Shift to clean fuels (EV, CNG)
3) Develop waste-to-energy (biogas, composting); Improve solid waste infrastructure; Promote circular economy practices; Implement scientific landfill systems
4) Restore mangrove ecosystems; Develop green corridors & urban parks; Promote nature-based solutions (NBS); Increase urban afforestation initiatives
5) Expand CAAQMS monitoring network; Implement clean air programs (NCAP); Reduce emissions from transport & industries
6) Upgrade stormwater drainage infrastructure; Implement flood risk management systems; Promote rainwater harvesting & reuse; Strengthen coastal resilience measures
strategies.info:
First Indian city aligned with C40 CAP framework
Strong focus on mitigation + adaptation
Includes climate budgeting and governance reforms
High reliance on public transport (~76%)

vision: Transition Nagpur into a low-emission, climate-resilient, green and inclusive city
goals:
Reduce GHG emissions through mitigation strategies
Enhance climate resilience of urban systems
Align with SDGs and Paris Agreement goals
Support low emission development pathway
organizations:
Nagpur Municipal Corporation (NMC)
Nagpur Smart & Sustainable City Development Corporation Ltd (NSSCDCL)
ICLEI South Asia
UN-Habitat
European Commission (funding support)
climateRisks:
Heat Waves
Urban Flooding
Drought / Water Scarcity
Hailstorms
riskFindings:
Heat Waves
Nagpur has witnessed extreme temperatures for 21 days (double the typical heat wave duration) in 2013. Temperatures have exceeded 45°C for seven consecutive summers. In the last 10 years, 196 cases of sunstroke were recorded and 18 resulted in deaths. Annual average temperature shows upward trend since 1981.

Urban Flooding
In 2018, Nagpur recorded 265mm of rainfall in just 9 hours, causing widespread flooding, loss of lives and property damage. Record highest 24-hr rainfall is 304 mm (July 12, 1994). Overall drainage coverage remains poor and inadequate despite regular cleaning.

Drought / Water Scarcity
Nagpur witnessed 12 incidents of droughts between 1901 and 1998 (per IMD). Annual average precipitation shows a decreasing trend (1989-2018).

Hailstorms
Nagpur district witnessed the highest number of hailstorm events (40) in India in the last 38 years (1972–2011), though frequency within the urban area is very low.

vision: Transform Rajkot into a climate-resilient, net-zero emissions city by 2070
goals: Transform Rajkot into a climate-resilient, net-zero emissions city by 2070
organizations:
Rajkot Municipal Corporation (RMC)
ICLEI South Asia
South Pole Group
econcept AG
National Institute of Urban Affairs (NIUA)
Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
Government of Gujarat
climateRisks:
Urban Heat
Urban Flooding
Water Scarcity
riskFindings:
Urban Heat
Rajkot is susceptible to urban heat as corroborated by Gujarat's composite risk index. Areas vulnerable to urban heat identified by analysing Land Surface Temperature (LST >40°C) and heat index (feels like temperature >36.2°C) for May. Vulnerable wards include 1, 2, parts of 3, 4, parts of 7, 9, 11, 12, 13, 17 and 18.

Urban Flooding
City is susceptible to urban flooding confirmed by Gujarat's State Disaster Management Plan 2016-17. Spatial analysis of flooding/waterlogging points and ward-level analysis of water and vector-borne diseases used to identify vulnerable areas.

vision: Transform Siliguri into a climate-resilient, sustainable city with net-zero emissions by 2070
goals:
Achieve Net Zero emissions by 2070
Reduce vulnerability to:
Urban heat
Urban flooding
organizations:
Siliguri Municipal Corporation (SMC)
ICLEI South Asia
South Pole Group
econcept AG
National Institute of Urban Affairs (NIUA)
Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
Government of West Bengal
riskFindings:
Urban Heat and Urban Flooding
Siliguri is susceptible to two climate risks: increased probability of extreme rainfall events (though average rainfall may decrease) and rising average temperatures — leading to urban heat and urban flooding as the two identified climate hazards.

Vulnerability Classification:
Wards 5, 33, 40, 41, 42 and 46 are 'extremely vulnerable' to both urban heat and urban flooding, with all urban systems (water supply, wastewater, solid waste, stormwater, health) being fragile. These 6 wards have a combined population of 194,784, of whom 50,571 (26%) are slum dwellers.

Wards 3, 6, 7, 11, 15, 18, 24, 25, 28, 35, 36 and 43 are 'moderately high vulnerable' — susceptible to urban heat and/or urban flooding, with two of four urban systems being fragile. These 12 wards house 188,709 people, of whom 41,576 (22%) are slum dwellers.

vision: Achieve Net Zero emissions with Climate Resilient development by 2070
goals:
Net Zero emissions by 2070
~95.16% emission reduction target
organizations:
Tiruchirappalli City Corporation (TCC)
ICLEI South Asia
South Pole Group
econcept AG
National Institute of Urban Affairs (NIUA)
Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
Government of Tamil Nadu
sectorContribution:
Stationary Energy (Buildings + Industry): -60–65%
Transport: -25–30%
Waste (solid + wastewater): -8–10%
climateRisks:
Urban Heat
Urban Flooding
Air Pollution
riskFindings:
Urban Heat
Risk areas include 8% of the city area, with feels-like temperature of more than 39.4°C impacting 23% of the population. 152 bus stations (55% of all bus stations) are in urban heat areas.

Urban Flooding
21% of the city area is impacted by urban flooding or waterlogging, affecting 0.23 million population. 32% of the total city area falls under flood-risk zones. Flooding poses a more significant threat to urban infrastructure than urban heat — especially to water supply and sewerage systems, and solid waste management.

Air Pollution
Areas impacted by high PM10 concentration (>60 µg/m³) identified; 3.8% of city area has elevated PM10 levels.

strategies.sector:

vision: Become a Net-Zero Climate Resilient City by 2070
goals: Become a Net-Zero Climate Resilient City by 2070
organizations:
Udaipur Municipal Corporation (UMC)
ICLEI South Asia
South Pole Group
econcept AG

Government of Rajasthan
sectorContribution:
Stationary Energy (Buildings + Industry): -69% (0.79 / 1.15)
Transport: -27% (0.31 / 1.15)
Waste + others: very small share
riskFindings:
Extreme Heat
Over half (57%) of the city area is designated as heat hotspots, with Land Surface Temperature (LST) exceeding the median of 37°C. This directly impacts 64% of the population (~356,016 people as of 2021). Temperatures in hotspot areas reach 42°C to 43°C. Key hotspot areas include Madri industrial area, MLSU and other educational institutions, RHB colonies in Hiran Magri, and congested residential developments on hillocks. Additionally, 76% of street vendors (2,117 people) and 29% of population (young children and elderly) are particularly vulnerable.

Urban Flooding
Udaipur's saucer-like topography creates a natural basin for water accumulation. 50 locations identified as prone to waterlogging during rainy season, especially near the Ayad River and city centre. Urban flooding affects ~13% of the city's total area, impacting 17% of the population. 5,537 slum dwellers (35% of total slum population) and 1,407 street vendors (51% of total) face heightened flood risks.

vision: Achieve Net Zero emissions by 2047 with a climate-resilient urban system
goals:
29% reduction by 2030 (vs BAU)
55% reduction by 2040
89% reduction by 2047
organizations:
Vijayawada Municipal Corporation (VMC)
ICLEI South Asia
C40 Cities (funding support)
Government of Andhra Pradesh
climateRisks:
Urban Flooding
Extreme Heat
Water Stress
Coastal / Cyclone Risk
riskFindings:
Urban Flooding
Vijayawada is highly vulnerable to flooding given its location along the Krishna River and its low-lying terrain.

Extreme Heat
Rising temperatures and increasing frequency of heat waves, compounded by urban heat island effects in densely built areas.

Water Stress
Pressure on water supply systems due to growing demand and climate variability.

Cyclone / Coastal Risk
Andhra Pradesh coast exposed to Bay of Bengal cyclones; city faces associated storm surge and wind damage risks.
